questions.text: In a min-heap, which relationship must hold between every node and its children?
choices: The node's value is less than or equal to both children's values
The node's value is greater than or equal to both children's values
The node's value must equal both children's values
There is no required relationship — only the shape matters
questions.explanation: The heap-order rule for a min-heap is: every node's value is $\le$ the values of both its children. This is what keeps the overall smallest value pinned at the root.

questions.text: Using the standard 0-indexed array representation of a heap, what is the index of the parent of the node at index 7?
In the array representation, the node at index 3 has two children. Write their indices as comma-separated values, left child first, no spaces, e.g. "1,2".
questions.explanation: Left child of index $i$ is $2i+1$, right child is $2i+2$. For $i = 3$: left $= 2(3)+1 = 7$, right $= 2(3)+2 = 8$.

questions.text: The heap shape rule requires the tree to be a "complete binary tree". What does that mean?
choices: Every level is completely filled, except possibly the last, which fills left to right with no gaps
Every node has exactly two children, with no exceptions
The tree is also fully sorted, left to right
Every leaf sits at exactly the same depth
questions.explanation: A complete binary tree fills every level fully except (possibly) the last, which is filled left to right leaving no gaps. This is exactly the property that lets a heap be packed into a plain array with no pointers.

questions.text: Which of the following are true of a binary heap?
choices: The root always holds the minimum value, in a min-heap
An in-order traversal of a min-heap always produces the values in sorted order
A heap can be stored efficiently as a plain array, with no left/right pointers
Every node's value must be less than every value in its right subtree
questions.explanation: The root is always the min (min-heap) or max (max-heap) — that's the whole point. But a heap is *not* a sorted structure like a BST: in-order traversal does not generally give sorted order, and the heap-order rule only constrains a node against its immediate children, not the whole subtree. Because the shape rule forces no gaps, the tree packs neatly into an array with no pointers needed.

questions.text: Starting from an empty min-heap, insert 6, 2, 9, 1 in that order (append, then sift up). What value ends up at index 0 of the final array?
questions.explanation: insert(6) → [6]. insert(2): append → [6,2]; parent of index 1 is index 0 ($6$); $2<6$, swap → [2,6]. insert(9): append → [2,6,9]; parent of index 2 is index 0 ($2$); $9<2$ is false, stop → [2,6,9]. insert(1): append → [2,6,9,1]; parent of index 3 is index 1 ($6$); $1<6$, swap → [2,1,9,6]; parent of index 1 is index 0 ($2$); $1<2$, swap → [1,2,9,6]. Final array: [1,2,9,6], so index 0 holds $1$.

questions.text: When extracting the root of a heap, which element is moved into the root position before sifting down?
choices: The last element in the array
The root's left child
A freshly re-hashed value
The smallest leaf in the tree
questions.explanation: Moving the last array element into the root keeps the tree's shape complete (one fewer node, still no gaps) with a single O(1) move; sift-down then restores the heap-order rule from the root downward.

questions.text: Extract the minimum from the min-heap array [2, 5, 4, 8, 9, 7] (replace the root with the last element, 7, then sift down). What value ends up at index 0 of the resulting array?
questions.explanation: Move the last element, 7, into the root and shrink: [7,5,4,8,9]. Sift down from index 0: children are index 1 ($5$) and index 2 ($4$); the smaller is $4$, and $7>4$, so swap → [4,5,7,8,9]. Now at index 2, both potential children (indices 5, 6) are beyond the array (length 5), so sifting stops. Final array: [4,5,7,8,9], index 0 holds $4$.

questions.text: Repeatedly extracting the minimum from a min-heap until it is empty always returns the values in ______ order. (One word.)
questions.explanation: Each extraction always returns whatever is currently smallest, so the sequence of extracted values is non-decreasing — ascending, sorted order. This is exactly the mechanism heapsort uses (with a max-heap, to build a list in ascending order by placing each maximum at the end).

questions.text: What is the true (not the naive per-node) time complexity of heapify (build-heap) on n elements?
choices: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
questions.explanation: Although each sift-down could naively be bounded at O(log n) and there are up to n of them, most nodes sit near the bottom of the tree with very little distance left to sift, so the total work sums to O(n), not O(n log n).

questions.text: Which of the following are true about heapsort?
choices: It sorts in place, using only O(1) extra space
Its overall time complexity is O(n log n)
It always builds and uses a min-heap
It needs a separate auxiliary array the size of the input, like merge sort
questions.explanation: Heapsort repeatedly swaps the root with the end of the shrinking unsorted region and sifts down, all within the original array — O(1) extra space, unlike merge sort's O(n). Its n extractions each cost O(log n), giving O(n log n) overall. The version described in this lesson uses a max-heap to build ascending order (though a min-heap variant producing descending order is equally possible) — it is not restricted to a min-heap, and it needs no separate auxiliary array.

questions.text: Which abstract data type does a binary heap efficiently implement, giving O(log n) insertion and O(log n) removal of the highest-priority item?
choices: Priority queue
Stack
FIFO queue
Hash table
questions.explanation: A priority queue always serves the highest-priority item next, regardless of arrival order. A binary heap is the standard implementation because both insert and pop stay at O(log n) — better than the O(n) a plain sorted or unsorted list would need for one of the two operations.
